title: Freestyle and Twill.ai: Infrastructure for Autonomous Coding Agents
excerpt: Two new platforms are building the cloud sandbox infrastructure that coding agents need to work autonomously, shifting AI coding tools from assistance to task delegation.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
You Want to Hand Off Code Tasks to an AI and Actually Trust the Result

The problem isn't finding an AI that can write code anymore. Cursor and GitHub Copilot do that well. The real friction happens when you try to go further. You need to delegate a full task - add Stripe billing, refactor this module, update the database schema - and step away. But current coding tools aren't built for that. They're designed to augment you while you watch. The moment you leave to focus on something else, the agent can't provision environments, install dependencies, run tests, or observe what's happening. It's still autocomplete wearing an agent costume.

TL;DR

Freestyle and Twill.ai are building the cloud infrastructure layer that coding agents actually need to work unsupervised. This shifts the entire market from assisted coding (you and AI working together) to delegated coding (you describe a task, AI delivers a PR).

The Real Bottleneck Nobody Was Talking About

Two platforms landed on Hacker News this week with nearly identical upvotes (Freestyle: 260, Twill.ai: 65) because they both spotted the same hidden constraint. Coding agents can't operate without infrastructure. They need a place to actually run.

Goose and OpenClaw proved the autonomous agent model could work. But they run locally, which means they're limited to your machine. The moment you want agents handling real projects with real dependencies, real network calls, and real test suites, you need something else. You need isolated sandboxes where an agent can thrash around safely without breaking your local setup or your codebase.

Without that infrastructure, agents are just very confident autocomplete. With it, they become something you can actually delegate to.

That's the gap Freestyle and Twill.ai are filling. They're not building better language models. They're building the plumbing that makes existing models actually useful for hands-off task delegation.

Freestyle: The Infrastructure Play

Freestyle positions itself as a cloud for coding agents. Spin up a sandbox, give an agent access, let it work. The agent gets a full Linux filesystem, package managers, network access, and the ability to run arbitrary code without polluting your environment.

The product is API-first. It's not trying to be the agent. It's trying to be what agents run on top of. You're not the customer. Teams building agent-powered developer tools are. That matters strategically because it means Freestyle isn't competing with Cursor or GitHub Copilot. It's betting that the real value is in becoming the foundational layer that everything else depends on.

The Hacker News reception (260 points) tells you developers see this as a genuine problem with a solution that actually works. This is infrastructure that was obviously needed the moment you thought about it.

Twill.ai: Same Problem, Different Angle

Twill.ai (YC Summer 2025) uses the same cloud sandbox infrastructure but wraps it in a different business model. Instead of selling sandboxes to developers, it sells outcomes. Post a task in Slack or Jira. Get back a pull request.

That's a much harder product problem. Freestyle just needs to provide reliable, isolated environments. Twill needs to make agents reliable enough that their PRs don't require line-by-line review. It needs to handle retries, escalation decisions, and knowing when to admit failure. The 65 Hacker News points reflect that it's solving a deeper problem: not just can agents run unsupervised, but should they?

DimensionFreestyleTwill.ai
CustomerDevelopers building agent toolsEngineering teams with tasks to delegate
What you getSandboxed environmentsPull requests
ComplexityInfrastructureJudgment and reliability

Why This Breaks the Current Product Conversation

Today when you compare Cursor vs GitHub Copilot, you're asking: whose model is smarter? Whose IDE integration is smoother? Whose subscription costs less?

That entire conversation becomes outdated once you have reliable execution infrastructure. The question stops being "which tool helps me code faster" and becomes "which tool can I trust to finish a task while I answer emails." Different tools for different jobs. Cursor excels at assisted, interactive coding. Autonomous agents with proper infrastructure excel at delegated, defined tasks.

The agents already exist. Goose and OpenClaw are open source. Claude Code can handle complex reasoning. The bottleneck was never the models. It was the lack of a safe place for them to actually execute.

Important

This infrastructure shift will determine which AI coding tools become essential to your workflow. Teams that build reliable execution layers will win. Teams that just focus on model quality will stay in the assistance category.

Step by Step: Evaluating Coding Tools for Your Team

The decision tree is now clearer. Ask these questions in order:

  1. Do you need assisted coding or delegated coding? Assisted means you work alongside the AI (Cursor, GitHub Copilot). Delegated means you hand off tasks and expect results (Twill, Freestyle-backed agents). Most teams need both for different workflows.
  2. If you need delegation, what infrastructure backs the agent? Local-only agents like Goose work for personal projects. For team usage, you need sandboxed cloud infrastructure that isolates tasks and allows reliable feedback loops.
  3. What's your tolerance for agent mistakes? Freestyle gives you control over the environment. Twill takes responsibility for the output quality. Different risk profiles.
  4. How integrated does this need to be with your existing workflow? Twill integrates with Slack, GitHub, and Linear. Freestyle is deeper in the stack. Choose based on where the friction actually is in your team's process.

2

categories of AI coding tools emerging: assisted (you work with it) and delegated (you hand it tasks)

The Market Shift Happening Right Now

The 2023-2024 wave of AI coding tools was about speed and augmentation. Write faster. Spot bugs faster. Understand code faster. Those tools are mature now. The new wave is about leverage and automation.

What that requires, beyond better models:

  • Isolated environments that agents can safely execute in without affecting your machine or codebase
  • Clear visibility into what the agent is doing and why it made decisions
  • Feedback mechanisms that let agents correct course without human intervention at every step
  • Integration with the tools your team already uses (version control, issue tracking, communication)

Freestyle and Twill.ai are building exactly these pieces. Neither is a household name and both operate at the infrastructure layer where most users never see them. But they're solving the constraint that has silently held autonomous coding back.

The companies that make autonomous coding actually reliable will be foundational, boring infrastructure. Nobody gets excited about that. Everyone depends on it anyway.

What Changes When This Works

Right now, when you need a feature built or a codebase refactored, you either do it yourself (slow) or hire someone (expensive). AI coding tools help with the first option but don't solve the underlying problem: you still have to be present and directing.

When execution infrastructure becomes reliable, you can actually delegate. File a ticket. Describe the task clearly. Let the agent work in its own sandbox. Review the PR when it's ready. For high-context, well-defined work, this works. For exploratory coding and design decisions, you'll still want the assisted model.

The winning teams won't choose one or the other. They'll use assisted tools like Cursor for collaborative work and delegated tools backed by solid infrastructure for clearly scoped tasks. That's the actual future.

Quick Verification Checklist

  • Determine whether your primary need is assisted coding (real-time collaboration with AI) or delegated coding (handing off complete tasks)
  • If delegated: verify the tool uses sandboxed infrastructure that isolates task execution from your production environment
  • Check that feedback loops exist for the agent to retry, escalate, or request clarification without manual intervention at every step
  • Confirm integration points with your existing workflow (Slack, GitHub, Jira, or whatever you actually use)
  • Test with a low-stakes task first - something clearly defined where you can validate the output quality
  • Document your team's guidelines for what types of tasks are safe to delegate versus what requires human collaboration
